cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Archimedes expected the planetarium to reproduce the model's predictions at credible accuracy (it did, on Cicero's testimony of being deeply impressed by it); to communicate the celestial-motion model to non-mathematician audiences (it did, with Cicero himself functioning as a documented non-mathematician audience member); to survive as a single-instance object through Marcellus's removal to Rome (it did, surviving at minimum until Cicero saw it in the early first century BC); and to demonstrate the operational possibility of gear-driven theoretical-model reproduction (it did, anticipating the Antikythera tradition by some decades).

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another astronomer with the same mathematical celestial-motion model — would in most cases have produced only textual mathematical work circulated among peer astronomers. Few peers would have constructed the gear-driven physical-instrument translation across communication channels, accepting the substantial engineering investment and producing a single-instance object whose operational value was its communication-channel function.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Archimedes's divergence rests on his cross-channel-translation discipline and on his perception of theoretical models as translatable into physical-instrument form for non-mathematician audiences. The same disposition recurs in his audience-calibrated correspondence, in his calibrated public demonstrations, and in his channel-bifurcation architecture generally.
critical_incident_database.outcome: The planetarium and a celestial globe were taken to Rome by Marcellus after the sack of Syracuse. Cicero saw the planetarium in Rome in the mid-first century BC and was deeply impressed by it, recording its operation in De Re Publica. The instrument's transmission and Cicero's documentation are the foundational evidence for the gear-driven astronomical-modeling tradition whose archaeological parallel is the Antikythera Mechanism.

critical_incident_database.decision: In Quadrature of the Parabola, Archimedes proved the same theorem (the parabolic-segment area equals 4/3 the inscribed triangle's area) twice — first by a mechanical-balance argument using indivisibles, then by a rigorous exhaustion-based geometric proof — providing in this single treatise the only published instance of his heuristic-and-proof channel-pairing during the body of his career.
critical_incident_database.context: The standard Archimedean publication pattern was rigorous-proof-only: the mechanical heuristic was kept private (incident 11), and the published treatises showed only the formal exhaustion-based geometric proofs. Quadrature of the Parabola is an exception to this pattern: it shows both modes of proof, with the mechanical-balance argument preceding the rigorous geometric proof in the treatise's exposition. The choice was a deliberate channel-disclosure within the body of his career rather than only at the late-life Method moment.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Archimedes attended to a structural feature of the parabolic-segment result that made it suitable for partial heuristic disclosure: the result was geometrically clean (a single fraction, 4/3), the heuristic argument was illustratively powerful, and the rigorous proof followed naturally from preliminary exhaustion-based work. He noticed that one limited disclosure in the body of his career would establish the heuristic-and-proof distinction without committing to comprehensive disclosure. He noticed that publishing both modes in a single treatise allowed the reader to see the relationship between heuristic and proof directly, making the channel-distinction explicit through example rather than through abstract methodological discussion.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Archimedes's operative goals were: (1) demonstrate the heuristic-and-proof channel-distinction through a single illustrative example; (2) preserve the comprehensive-disclosure decision for late-career deployment (which would arrive with The Method); (3) produce the parabolic-segment result through both modes of proof so that the relationship between them was visibly accessible; (4) calibrate the partial disclosure to a single result rather than to a comprehensive methodological essay; (5) embed the partial disclosure within the standard treatise architecture so that it functioned as channel-credentialed mathematics rather than as methodological controversy.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: Archimedes rejected the alternative of comprehensive heuristic disclosure within Quadrature of the Parabola, recognizing that comprehensive disclosure would have foreclosed the deliberate late-career disclosure. He rejected the alternative of strict heuristic-concealment in this treatise, recognizing that the parabolic-segment result was uniquely well-suited for the partial disclosure as illustrative instance. He rejected the alternative of a separate methodological essay, recognizing that channel-orthodoxy was preserved by embedding the partial disclosure in a substantive treatise rather than in a freestanding methodological work.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Archimedes framed the parabolic-segment treatise as a partial-disclosure intervention within his channel-architectural sequence: a single illustrative instance now, with comprehensive disclosure deferred to the structural moment of late-career release. The frame is the same channel-architectural calibration that recurs across his career, treating disclosure-timing and disclosure-scope as structurally optimizable variables.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Archimedes expected the partial disclosure to establish the heuristic-and-proof distinction as a recognized methodological feature of his work (it did, with subsequent commentators including Heath identifying Quadrature of the Parabola as the precedent for The Method's later comprehensive disclosure); the channel-orthodoxy of the rigorous proof to preserve the treatise's reception (it did); and the deferred comprehensive disclosure to remain available for late-career deployment (it did, with The Method).

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another mathematician with a private heuristic and a parabolic-quadrature result to publish — would in most cases have either disclosed only the rigorous proof (omitting the heuristic) or disclosed only the heuristic (collapsing into channel-departure). Few peers would have constructed the calibrated partial disclosure that paired both modes in a single treatise as a precedent-establishing instance.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Archimedes's divergence rests on his disclosure-timing-and-scope optimization and on his channel-architectural insight that partial disclosures can establish methodological precedent without comprehensive commitment. The same disposition recurs across his work in the calibrated timing of public demonstrations, in his deferred late-career Method release, and in his audience-calibrated patronal correspondence.
critical_incident_database.outcome: Quadrature of the Parabola survived in the standard manuscript tradition and is recognized in modern scholarship (Heath, Dijksterhuis) as the precedent-establishing instance of Archimedes's heuristic-and-proof channel-pairing. The treatise's pairing of the two modes is one of the principal pieces of evidence for the lifelong channel-separation discipline that The Method later confirmed.

critical_incident_database.decision: At the moment of his death during the Roman sack of Syracuse in autumn 212 BC, Archimedes — by all transmitted accounts — was either working on a geometric diagram, carrying mathematical instruments, or moving to deliver them to Marcellus, and refused to interrupt the work despite the immediate physical threat from the soldier — preserving the working mode through the operational extreme of the city's fall.
critical_incident_database.context: Marcellus had given specific orders that Archimedes was to be taken alive and protected. The orders were not followed by the individual soldier who encountered him during the sack. Plutarch transmits three variant versions of the encounter: working on a diagram and refusing to leave it; carrying instruments in a box that the soldier suspected of treasure; approaching Marcellus to surrender his instruments. All variants converge on the structural feature of working-mode persistence into the moment of death.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Across the three transmitted variants, what is consistent is Archimedes's perception of the encounter not as a defensive emergency requiring immediate operational reconfiguration but as an interruption of the working mode that could be addressed once the immediate work was completed (in the diagram variant) or that called for delivery of mathematical instruments (in the other variants). He noticed (or his lifelong working-mode pattern reflexively continued perceiving) that the working mode was constitutive of his identity, sustained across all prior crises including the two-year siege, and that conversion to defensive-emergency mode at the moment of the city's fall was not the operational frame he had been operating in.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Archimedes's operative goals were: (1) complete the immediate piece of work he was engaged in; (2) preserve the working mode through the moment of physical encounter; (3) avoid converting to defensive-emergency mode whose operational return was negligible (defensive-emergency mode would not have produced effective resistance against an armed Roman soldier); (4) treat the encounter within the framework of the patron-relationship-and-courtesy architecture that Marcellus's protective order had established; (5) absorb the personal-risk cost of working-mode persistence as the consistent operational consequence of his lifelong working architecture.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: Archimedes rejected the alternative of immediate flight or defensive action upon encountering the soldier, recognizing that flight or defense against an armed Roman would have been operationally futile and would have required conversion to a mode he had never operated in. He rejected the alternative of pre-emptively abandoning the working mode at the start of the sack and seeking concealment, recognizing that the working mode had been sustained through the entire two-year siege and that abandonment at this final moment would have been inconsistent with the lifelong architecture. He rejected the alternative of abandoning the immediate work to plead with the soldier on protective-order grounds, recognizing that the soldier's protective-order awareness could not be ascertained.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Archimedes framed the encounter not as a defensive emergency requiring mode-conversion but as an interruption to be addressed once the immediate work was completed. The frame is the same working-mode persistence that recurs through the entire two-year siege and across his preceding career, sustained even into the operational extreme of the city's fall. The frame produced the working-on-the-diagram circumstances that the surviving accounts unanimously transmit.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Archimedes did not, on the surviving evidence, expect the encounter to terminate fatally. He expected (in the diagram variant) the soldier to wait until he had completed his proof; he expected (in the instrument variant) to deliver his instruments. The actual outcome — the soldier's failure to follow Marcellus's protective order, the killing — was a contingent operational accident rather than an expected terminus. The structural pattern preserved across the variants is not a calculated acceptance of death but the persistence of the working mode that defined Archimedes's productive architecture across his entire career.

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another civilian intellectual encountered by an armed soldier during the sack of a fallen city — would in most cases have prioritized survival over immediate work, complying with the soldier's directives, abandoning materials, and requesting protective escort. Few peers would have sustained the working mode through the encounter to the degree that Archimedes is recorded as having sustained it across all three variant accounts.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Archimedes's divergence rests on his lifelong working-mode persistence and on his consistent channel-separation between productive activity and external-environmental conditions. The capacity to preserve the working mode through the operational extreme is the same disposition that recurs in his theoretical-channel persistence through the siege period, in his channel-separation between engineering and theoretical work, and in his lifelong refusal to allow external conditions to constitute the productive architecture.
critical_incident_database.outcome: Archimedes was killed by the Roman soldier, despite Marcellus's protective order. Marcellus is recorded as having grieved at the death and as having considered Archimedes 'a friend.' The death-during-working-mode episode became the canonical closing image of Archimedes's career and was reproduced across ancient and Renaissance literature as the representative instance of the philosopher's persistence of work under operational threat.

critical_incident_database.decision: In Book II of On Floating Bodies, Archimedes investigated the stability of paraboloid segments floating in fluid — computing the conditions under which a segment of given specific gravity would float upright on its base versus tilting onto its side — at a level of technical demand widely regarded as among the most difficult work of antiquity, accepting the channel-cost of producing technically demanding work that few subsequent readers would be able to follow.
critical_incident_database.context: On Floating Bodies Book I had established the foundational principle of buoyancy at moderate technical demand. Book II addressed the substantially harder problem of stability — when a paraboloidal segment floats, will it remain upright or tilt? The question had direct engineering relevance to ship hull design (Archimedes had presumably worked through stability questions during the Syracusia consultation) but the technical analysis required to address it operated at the upper limit of Hellenistic mathematical capacity.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Archimedes attended to a structural feature of the stability problem that made it operationally important regardless of its technical demand: floating-body stability is the load-bearing question for ship hull design, and treating it inadequately would have left the most engineering-relevant question of hydrostatics unaddressed. He noticed that the technical demand of the analysis was not optional — the stability conditions cannot be derived without the substantial preliminary work on centers of gravity, on geometric properties of paraboloid segments, and on the integration of these results — and that producing the result required absorbing the technical demand into the treatise's deductive structure. He noticed that few subsequent readers would have the technical capacity to follow the Book II analysis, but that the channel-credentialing did not require comprehensive readership; it required that the work be present in the channel for the readers competent to use it.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Archimedes's operative goals were: (1) produce the rigorous stability analysis that the engineering-relevant question required; (2) absorb the technical demand into the deductive structure rather than presenting it as virtuosity or excluding it for accessibility; (3) preserve the channel-architectural decision to publish into Alexandria the technically-demanding theoretical work regardless of broad readability; (4) construct the analysis at the rigor level the geometric tradition required; (5) accept the channel-cost of producing work whose readership would be narrow even within the credentialing community.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: Archimedes rejected the alternative of declining to address the stability problem, recognizing that omission would have left the engineering-relevant question of hydrostatics unaddressed. He rejected the alternative of treating the problem at lesser technical demand for accessibility, recognizing that the result could not be obtained without the technical depth. He rejected the alternative of separating the stability analysis into a separate work to keep On Floating Bodies Book I more accessible, recognizing that the integrated treatise architecture preserved the relationship between the foundational principle (Book I) and its application to engineering-relevant cases (Book II).
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Archimedes framed the stability analysis as the operationally-required application of the foundational principle, regardless of the technical demand absorption required. The frame is the same operational-sufficiency calibration that recurs across his work — produce exactly the analysis the operational requirement demands, regardless of channel-receptional cost or accessibility cost. The frame produced the technically demanding Book II that subsequent commentators (Heath, Dijksterhuis) have identified as among the most difficult work of antiquity.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Archimedes expected the stability analysis to address the engineering-relevant question (it did); the technical demand to limit the readership even within the credentialing community (it did); the channel-credentialing to be preserved by the rigorous-proof discipline (it was); and the work to survive in the standard manuscript tradition for the readers competent to use it (it did, through the Moerbeke translation and the palimpsest).

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another investigator of hydrostatic stability — would in most cases have either declined the stability problem (omission) or treated it at lesser technical demand (loss of rigor). Few peers would have produced the rigorous stability analysis at the technical depth that On Floating Bodies Book II exhibits.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Archimedes's divergence rests on his operational-sufficiency-regardless-of-channel-cost discipline and on his perception of technical demand as a feature to be absorbed into deductive structure rather than as a constraint on what can be analyzed. The same disposition recurs in his other technically demanding work (the paraboloid-area computations, the spiral analysis, the centers-of-gravity work).
critical_incident_database.outcome: On Floating Bodies Book II survived in the manuscript tradition and is recognized in modern scholarship as among the most technically demanding work of antiquity. The stability analysis anticipated subsequent ship-hull-design theory by nearly two thousand years and is structurally consistent with modern naval architecture's stability principles.

critical_incident_database.decision: When Conon of Samos died in Archimedes's correspondence-channel (probably c. 240s BC), Archimedes did not allow the channel to lapse but instead transferred the addressee role to Dositheus of Pelusium — preserving channel-durability through a structural-personnel discontinuity by deliberate succession-relationship construction, treating the channel itself as the load-bearing operational asset rather than any single correspondent.
critical_incident_database.context: Conon was Archimedes's principal Alexandrian correspondent during the early-and-middle period of his career — the addressee of On the Sphere and Cylinder Book I and several other major treatises. Conon's death foreclosed the personal correspondence channel as constituted. The structural choice was whether to allow the channel to lapse (returning to an institutional-channel mode for subsequent publications) or to transfer the channel to a successor correspondent.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Archimedes attended to a structural feature of his publication architecture that channel-lapse would have foreclosed: the personal-correspondence channel was the load-bearing operational asset of his theoretical-publication architecture (incident 3), and its lapse would have required reconstructing the entire channel-architecture or returning to less-credentialing institutional modes. He noticed that channel-durability could be sustained through correspondent-succession if a competent successor was available and willing to assume the addressee role. He noticed that Dositheus of Pelusium was operationally available as successor — a competent mathematician at Alexandria who had been adjacent to the Conon correspondence and could continue the channel without requiring channel-architectural reconstruction.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Archimedes's operative goals were: (1) preserve the personal-correspondence-channel architecture across Conon's death; (2) identify and engage Dositheus as successor addressee; (3) explicitly acknowledge the channel-transfer in the prefaces of subsequent treatises so that the documentary record preserved the continuity; (4) calibrate the prose register to Dositheus's competence (which was peer-mathematician but distinct from Conon's specific expertise); (5) treat the channel itself as the operational asset rather than treating any single correspondent as constitutive.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: Archimedes rejected the alternative of allowing the channel to lapse and returning to institutional-channel publication, recognizing that channel-architectural reconstruction would have been substantially more costly than succession. He rejected the alternative of a silent transfer (changing addressees without explicit acknowledgment), recognizing that explicit acknowledgment in the prefaces preserved the documentary continuity that the channel-architecture required. He rejected the alternative of dividing subsequent work between multiple successor correspondents, recognizing that the single-successor structure preserved the channel's coherence.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Archimedes framed the post-Conon situation not as a structural-architectural failure but as a succession event within an enduring channel-architecture. The frame deliberately separated the personal-correspondent (Conon, particular) from the channel-architecture (general, transferable) and treated the latter as the load-bearing asset to be preserved. The frame is the same channel-durability discipline that recurs across his work in the long-arc capacity-construction architecture and in the patronal-relationship maintenance with Hiero.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Archimedes expected the channel-transfer to preserve channel-durability across Conon's death (it did, with subsequent treatises addressed to Dositheus across the remainder of the relevant period); the documentary continuity to be preserved through the explicit preface acknowledgments (it was — the prefaces survive in the manuscript tradition and are the principal evidence for the succession); and the channel-architecture to remain operationally available for late-career deployment (it did, with The Method addressed to Eratosthenes in a further succession event near the end of his life).

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another mathematician whose principal correspondent had died — would in most cases have allowed the personal-channel to lapse, treating Conon's death as the natural terminus of the correspondence and shifting subsequent publications to whatever institutional channel was available. Few peers would have constructed the deliberate succession-relationship that Archimedes constructed with Dositheus and would have explicitly acknowledged the transfer as a channel-durability instrument.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Archimedes's divergence rests on his channel-durability discipline and his perception of channel-architecture as a structurally distinct operational asset separable from any single correspondent. The same disposition recurs in his patron-relationship architecture (preserved across Hiero's reign and partially across his death), in his theoretical-publication architecture (sustained across the entire productive period), and in his correspondence-channel architecture (sustained across multiple correspondent-successions including the late-career transfer to Eratosthenes).
critical_incident_database.outcome: Archimedes addressed his subsequent treatises to Dositheus following Conon's death, with explicit preface acknowledgment of the channel-transfer. The channel-architecture sustained through Conon's death without lapse, and the documentary continuity is preserved in the surviving prefaces.

critical_incident_database.decision: Across his published treatises, Archimedes calibrated the length of each work precisely to the structural complexity of its result — Measurement of a Circle remains very short (three propositions) because the π bounds can be obtained tightly, while On the Sphere and Cylinder is comparatively long because the sphere-cylinder result requires extensive preliminary work; declining to inflate short results into long treatises or compress long results into short ones.
critical_incident_database.context: Hellenistic mathematical-treatise composition had no established length convention. Some writers padded short results with extensive preliminary discussion; others compressed substantial results into terse expositions that depended on prior knowledge. The compositional choice was not fixed by genre and was therefore a structurally optimizable variable. Archimedes's pattern, visible across his surviving corpus, is precise calibration of length to complexity.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Archimedes attended to a structural feature of treatise-length composition that natural-prose composition would not have produced: the operational-utility profile of a treatise depends on its length-to-complexity ratio, with short treatises serving as operational reference (excerptable, memorable, transmissible) and long treatises serving as comprehensive deductive demonstration. He noticed that a short result inflated into a long treatise loses the operational-reference utility without gaining demonstration depth, while a long-required result compressed into short treatise loses the demonstration depth without gaining operational-reference utility. He noticed that calibration of length to result-complexity preserved both functions across the corpus.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Archimedes's operative goals were: (1) calibrate each treatise's length precisely to its result's structural complexity; (2) preserve the operational-reference utility of short treatises (Measurement of a Circle, Quadrature of the Parabola, The Sand Reckoner) by maintaining their compactness; (3) preserve the demonstration-depth utility of long treatises (On the Sphere and Cylinder, On Floating Bodies, On Spirals) by allowing them their required length; (4) maintain channel-credentialing across the corpus through consistent rigor regardless of length; (5) optimize the corpus's transmission durability by producing the mix of short and long treatises that the underlying results required.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: Archimedes rejected the alternative of uniform length across treatises, recognizing that uniform length would have produced inflation in some cases and compression in others. He rejected the alternative of pursuing length to display virtuosity, recognizing that virtuosity-driven inflation would have lost the operational-reference utility of short results. He rejected the alternative of compressing long results to display economy, recognizing that compression would have lost demonstration depth.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Archimedes framed treatise-length as a structurally optimizable variable calibrated to result-complexity rather than as a stylistic preference or institutional convention. The frame is the same operational-sufficiency discipline that recurs across his work — produce exactly the structure that the underlying result requires, no more and no less. The frame produced the treatise corpus whose mix of short and long works has been a structural feature of its transmission durability.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Archimedes expected the short treatises to maximize transmission durability through their operational-reference utility (they did — Measurement of a Circle was among the most widely transmitted Archimedean works through the medieval period); the long treatises to preserve demonstration depth (they did, with On the Sphere and Cylinder maintaining its full deductive structure across the manuscript transmission); and the corpus's channel-credentialing to be preserved by consistent rigor regardless of length (it was).

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another mathematician composing a corpus of treatises — would in most cases have either pursued uniform length (producing inflation in some cases and compression in others) or composed without deliberate length-calibration (producing arbitrary length variation). Few peers would have calibrated length precisely to result-complexity across the entire corpus as Archimedes did.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Archimedes's divergence rests on his operational-sufficiency calibration extended to compositional structure. The same disposition recurs in his π bounds (calibrated to operational accuracy), in his Sand Reckoner notation (calibrated to operational requirement), and in his engineering practice (calibrated to operational robustness rather than peak performance).
critical_incident_database.outcome: The Archimedean corpus exhibits a structurally distinctive mix of short and long treatises, each calibrated to its result's complexity. The mix has been a structural feature of the corpus's transmission durability, with the short treatises preserved through their operational-reference utility and the long treatises preserved through their demonstration depth.

critical_incident_database.decision: Archimedes selected, for his most spectacular public engineering demonstrations across his career — the compound-pulley Syracusia launch, the bronze planetarium, the war engines of Syracuse, and the crown-problem investigation — only those occasions on which the demonstration value coincided with an actual operational requirement, declining to produce purely contrived demonstrations as standalone exhibitions of theoretical capacity.
critical_incident_database.context: Archimedes's reputation across the Hellenistic and Roman world rested substantially on a small number of spectacular engineering demonstrations. The cumulative selection pattern, visible across the surviving record, is that each spectacular demonstration occurred on an occasion of actual operational requirement (the Syracusia required launching; Hiero required forensic investigation of the crown; the war engines addressed an actual military threat; the planetarium served as Hiero's prestige object and possibly as astronomical-instruction tool). Purely contrived theatrical exhibitions of theoretical capacity — common in Hellenistic court entertainment — are absent from the surviving Archimedean record.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Archimedes attended to a structural feature of public-demonstration reception that purely contrived exhibitions would have weakened: the operational-utility coincidence anchors the demonstration in real-world consequence, while purely contrived exhibitions are received as entertainment whose theoretical claim is taken less seriously. He noticed that the rare-but-high-yield demonstration profile of his career depended on each demonstration's coupling to operational requirement; producing additional contrived demonstrations would have inflated the frequency curve without producing structural-yield commensurate with the channel-cost. He noticed that the conservation of public-demonstration-channel rarity preserved the weighting of each individual demonstration when deployed.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Archimedes's operative goals were: (1) couple every public demonstration to an actual operational requirement; (2) preserve the rarity of public demonstrations as a structural amplifier of their weighting; (3) decline contrived theatrical exhibitions that Hellenistic court culture would have rewarded; (4) sustain the channel-discipline against patron pressure to produce additional demonstrations beyond the operational-requirement-coupled occasions; (5) optimize the frequency curve of public engagement for maximum structural-yield per demonstration.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: Archimedes rejected the alternative of producing additional purely contrived demonstrations as standalone exhibitions, recognizing that contrived demonstrations would have inflated frequency without producing structural-yield. He rejected the alternative of declining all public demonstrations to preserve total privacy of theoretical work, recognizing that the rare-but-coupled demonstration profile produced operationally valuable structural-yield. He rejected the alternative of allowing patron pressure to determine demonstration frequency, recognizing that channel-discipline required Archimedes's own selection of operational-requirement coincidence.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Archimedes framed the demonstration-frequency profile as a structurally optimizable variable governed by operational-requirement coincidence rather than by patron preference or court-entertainment expectations. The frame is the same calibrated-public-facing-channel-conservation discipline that recurs across his work in audience-calibrated correspondence, in deferred late-career disclosures, and in selective theoretical-channel publication.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Archimedes expected the rare-but-high-yield demonstration profile to produce maximum cumulative reputational capital from minimum demonstrations (it did — his reputation rested on a handful of spectacular demonstrations rather than on a catalog of court entertainments); each demonstration's weighting in subsequent reportage to be amplified by the rarity of the channel (it was — Plutarch, Athenaeus, Pappus, and others reproduce the same small set of demonstrations as the canonical instances); and the channel-discipline to be sustained against patron pressure across forty years (it was).

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another engineer in Archimedes's structural position — would in most cases have produced additional contrived demonstrations as standalone exhibitions, particularly under patron pressure for court entertainment. Few peers would have sustained the operational-requirement-coupling discipline across forty years and would have produced the rare-but-high-yield demonstration profile that Archimedes produced.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Archimedes's divergence rests on his calibrated-public-facing-channel-conservation discipline and on his perception of demonstration-frequency as a structurally optimizable variable. The same disposition recurs across his work — rarity of public correspondence channel deployments, conservation of late-career disclosure timing, channel-bifurcation between engineering-occasion outputs and theoretical-channel publications.
critical_incident_database.outcome: Archimedes's reputation across the Hellenistic and Roman world rested on a small number of spectacular demonstrations, each coupled to an actual operational requirement, with the rarity of the demonstrations amplifying their reputational weighting in the historical reception. The channel-discipline sustained across forty years of patronage and is the canonical instance of his calibrated-public-facing-channel-conservation pattern.
